one_line_takeaway: PIFE hardens an AI-text detector by feeding it explicit text-vs-canonical-form discrepancy metrics (Levenshtein, cosine similarity, BLEU, WER, Jaccard), sustaining 82.6% TPR@1%FPR under sentence-level attacks where conventional adversarial training collapses to 48.8%.
key_finding: Conventional adversarial training gives only 'superficial' robustness: it survives low-level syntactic noise but collapses against meaning-preserving semantic attacks. Under sentence-level All Mix at a strict 1% FPR, the adversarially-trained ModernBERT's TPR falls to 48.8% (and 51.2% for pure paraphrase) - the 'semantic evasion threshold.' In stark contrast, the PIFE-augmented model, by explicitly quantifying the discrepancy between text and its canonical form, sustains 82.6% TPR@1%FPR under sentence-level All Mix and 85.4% under paraphrase, remaining robust across all attack levels. The takeaway: explicitly engineering features from perturbation artifacts beats implicitly learning them via data augmentation. Zero-shot detectors, though competitive on clean data (Binoculars AUROC 0.985), essentially break under adversarial mixes (e.g., sentence All Mix AUROC 0.396-0.553).
core_contribution: The paper's own central contribution is PIFE (Perturbation-Invariant Feature Engineering), a defense framework for AI-generated-text detection that, instead of merely augmenting training with adversarial examples, explicitly models adversarial artifacts. PIFE first passes an input text X through a normalization function N(.) to produce a canonical form X', then computes a 'discrepancy vector' v_d of comparative metrics between X and X' (cosine similarity of sentence embeddings, Levenshtein distance, Jaccard index, BLEU, and Word Error Rate). This vector is concatenated with the token embeddings and fed to a ModernBERT classifier, which learns end-to-end (with no explicit attack label) to associate perturbation-magnitude patterns with the Human/AI origin label. Supporting contributions: (1) a comparative baseline of seven Transformer encoders to select an optimal detector backbone; (2) a hierarchical (character/word/sentence-level) attack taxonomy used to stress-test both a conventionally adversarially-trained ModernBERT and the PIFE-augmented model; and (3) the empirical identification of a 'semantic evasion threshold' where standard adversarial training collapses against meaning-preserving attacks while PIFE does not. Note: SCRN, DetectGPT/Fast-DetectGPT, DAMAGE, retrieval-based defenses (Krishna et al.), and the zero-shot detectors (Binoculars, Glimpse, LogRank) are CITED prior/related work used for context and comparison, NOT contributions of this paper.
threat_model: White-box adversary with full access to the target detector's architecture and parameters, used deliberately to stress-test worst-case (maximally-informed) robustness. The adversary applies meaning-preserving perturbations to AI-generated text to evade detection, organized into a three-level hierarchy: (1) character-level (deletion, insertion, swap, homoglyph substitution, invisible characters, keyboard typos, punctuation, upper/lower case, and an 'All Mix'); (2) word-level (synonym/antonym replacement, word deletion/insertion, word reordering, All Mix); and (3) sentence-level (paraphrasing, tense altering, sentence reordering/splitting/fusion, All Mix). Paraphrasing is singled out as the strongest evasion technique. The deployment context is high-stakes (academic integrity, disinformation detection), so the paper emphasizes low-FPR operating points because false positives (mislabeling human text as AI) carry high individual/social cost.
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) the evaluated attack set is representative but not exhaustive, so more advanced attacks could further degrade performance; (2) experiments are limited to English text in a few domains, leaving other languages and specialized genres (legal, scientific) untested; (3) adversarial training was performed in a single batch rather than iteratively. Reviewer-added observations grounded in the text: the white-box PIFE robustness is demonstrated only against static perturbation attacks - the paper itself notes (future work) that adaptive, query-based black-box attacks that learn to minimize PIFE's discrepancy features are untested and could be a direct weakness. Generalization is a known trade-off: the supervised detector is trained/tested on the same 13 in-domain LLMs, and the paper concedes performance may degrade on unseen generators (cross-model generalization is left to future work). A minor citation-numbering inconsistency appears in the Related Work text (reference [6] is described as SCRN but the reference list entry [6] points to a different paper) - this is the authors' bibliographic slip, not a substantive result issue.

datasets_used: CLEF 2024 PAN Generative-AI-Authorship shared task dataset (U.S. news headlines from 2021)
1,087 human-written samples and 14,131 AI-generated samples from 13 distinct LLMs (each LLM contributed 1,087 samples)

13 generator LLMs include alpaca-7b, bloomz-7b1, chavinlo-alpaca-13b, gemini-pro, gpt-3.5-turbo-0125, gpt-4-turbo-preview, llama-2-7b, llama-2-70b, mistral-7b-instruct, mixtral-8x7b, qwen1.5-72b, text-bison-002, vicgalle-gpt2-open-instruct-v1
Stratified 70% train / 20% validation / 10% test split; full corpus additionally perturbed with the adversarial attack suite
quantitative_results: Baseline (non-adversarial) best model ModernBERT: AUROC 0.994, TPR@1%FPR 0.943, TPR@3% 0.955, TPR@5% 0.973
ModernBERT class-wise: Human F1 0.897, AI F1 0.992, overall accuracy 0.985
Adversarially-trained ModernBERT, sentence-level All Mix: TPR@1%FPR 0.488, AUROC 0.921 (the 'semantic evasion threshold')
Adversarially-trained ModernBERT, pure paraphrase: TPR@1%FPR 0.512, AUROC 0.935
Adversarially-trained ModernBERT, word-level All Mix: TPR@1%FPR 0.533, AUROC 0.940; homoglyph TPR@1%FPR 0.435, AUROC 0.967
PIFE-augmented ModernBERT, sentence-level All Mix: TPR@1%FPR 0.826, AUROC 0.974
PIFE-augmented ModernBERT, pure paraphrase: TPR@1%FPR 0.854, AUROC 0.981
PIFE-augmented ModernBERT, word-level All Mix: TPR@1%FPR 0.887, AUROC 0.988; character-level All Mix: TPR@1%FPR 0.912, AUROC 0.992
Zero-shot on clean data: Binoculars AUROC 0.985 (TPR@1% 0.857), FastDetectGPT 0.972, Glimpse 0.965, LogRank 0.921
Zero-shot under sentence-level All Mix: Binoculars AUROC 0.553, FastDetectGPT 0.492, Glimpse 0.474, LogRank 0.396 (near-total collapse)
baselines_compared: Seven encoder backbones for detector selection: BERT (110M), DistilBERT (66M), RoBERTa (125M), XLNET (117M), ALBERT (12M), DeBERTa (184M), ModernBERT (149M)
Two defense strategies: conventional single-batch adversarial training on ModernBERT vs. the proposed PIFE-augmented ModernBERT
Four zero-shot detectors as external reference: FastDetectGPT, Glimpse, Binoculars, LogRank (LRR variant)
